role_summary: Educator and civil rights organizer; founder of the Highlander Folk School Citizenship Schools (1957), which trained thousands of Black Southerners in literacy and civic preparation; the curriculum was adopted by SCLC and run by Dorothy Foreman Cotton (entry 29).
ai_reading: Clark is named by at least eight corpus voices, a wider footprint than her single scholarly citation would predict, and the spread is itself the embedding-relevant observation: the speakers who invoke her do not cluster in one organizational lineage. Dorothy Foreman Cotton (entry 29) directed the Citizenship Education Program Clark founded and gives the most extended account; Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan (entry 11) knew her as Highlander colleagues and Charleston neighbors; Euvester Simpson (entry 41) and William Saunders (entry 130) met her as students passing through Highlander; Scott Bates (entry 116), Betty Garman Robinson (entry 7), William S. Leventhal (entry 129), and Mildred Bond Roxborough (entry 90) reference her in passing. The recurring anchor across these otherwise unrelated testimonies is Highlander Folk School and Esau Jenkins's John's Island work, which is where the archive locates her rather than at SCLC's organizational center, even though SCLC adopted her program.
biographical_paragraph: Septima Clark was not interviewed for the Civil Rights History Project; this catalog page treats her as an external figure whose presence in the archive is mediated through other voices. At least eight corpus interviewees name her, among them Dorothy Foreman Cotton (entry 29), Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan (entry 11), Euvester Simpson (entry 41), William Saunders (entry 130), Scott Bates (entry 116), and Betty Garman Robinson (entry 7); the verbatim passages are collected in the snippets below. The canonical scholarly source the catalog cites for biographical orientation appears as [src: 1] below.

interview_snippets.speaker: Dorothy Foreman Cotton
interview_snippets.lead_in: Dorothy Foreman Cotton, who would go on to direct SCLC's Citizenship Education Program, recalls being sent by Dr. King to Highlander Folk School and meeting Clark there for the first time:
interview_snippets.quote: I went to Highlander and I met this woman and Septima [Poinsette] Clark. And because Septima [Poinsette] Clark was working there at the Highlander folk school.

interview_snippets.speaker: Dorothy Foreman Cotton
interview_snippets.lead_in: Cotton describes traveling the South with Clark and Andrew Young to run the citizenship workshops, dividing the teaching among the three of them:
interview_snippets.quote: Septima [Poinsette] Clark and especially Septima [Poinsette] Clark and Andrew Young and I used to, well, we would, the three of us in a car. And I think people would sometimes think we were mother, son, and daughter. Septima [Poinsette] Clark being the elder, you know, in the car.

interview_snippets.speaker: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan
interview_snippets.lead_in: The Carawans, Highlander's music staff, knew Clark first as colleagues and later as Charleston neighbors; here they place her at the center of their own formation:
interview_snippets.quote: Septima Clark was a huge influence on both of us, just special kind of person.

interview_snippets.speaker: Euvester Simpson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Euvester Simpson, recalling a two-week citizenship workshop at Highlander, names the teaching staff she learned under:
interview_snippets.quote: And Septima Clark was one of our teachers, and there was a man who was an Esau Jenkins,

interview_snippets.speaker: William Saunders
interview_snippets.lead_in: William Saunders, a John's Island organizer, was present at Highlander during the 1959 state raid and recounts Clark's arrest:
interview_snippets.quote: In 1959, I went to Highlander Research Center, the Highlander Research Center in Monteagle, Tennessee. And they raided the place while I was there. And they arrested Septima Clark.

sources.title: Cynthia Stokes Brown (ed.), Ready From Within: Septima Clark and the Civil Rights Movement
